mnemonics.title: PEAS Components
mnemonics.content: Remember PEAS as 'People Eat Apples Safely' - Performance measure, Environment, Actuators, Sensors. These define the task environment for an agent.

mnemonics.title: Agent Types Order
mnemonics.content: Simple → Goal → Utility → Learning. Think: 'Sensors Guide Useful Learning' - Reflex (Simple), Goal-based, Utility-based, Learning agents in increasing order of sophistication.

mnemonics.title: Rationality Definition
mnemonics.content: R.A.P.E. = Rational Agent Performance measure Evaluation - Rational agents maximize expected performance measure based on available information.

mnemonics.title: Rational vs Omniscient
mnemonics.content: Rational = Right decision with available info; Omniscient = Right decision knowing all outcomes. Rationality is achievable, omniscience is not.

acronyms.usage: Framework for specifying task environments for agents
How much the agent can perceive about its environment

visualTips.visualization: Draw a flow: Sensors → Percepts → Knowledge Base → Reasoning → Actions → Actuators → Environment. The Knowledge Base is central to rational decision-making.

Create a table with four columns: Performance (criteria), Environment (where it operates), Actuators (what it can do), Sensors (what it perceives). Think of a robot vacuum: Clean (P), Home floor (E), Wheels/motor (A), Cameras/bumpers (S).
Draw a pyramid: Bottom = Simple Reflex (if-then), Middle = Goal-based (search/planning), Top = Utility-based (utility function), Peak = Learning (improves over time).

analogies.analogy: Rationality is like a human driver using GPS and road signs to decide the best route. Omniscience would be already knowing exactly which routes have accidents, construction, and traffic - impossible knowledge.

A rational agent is like a chess player who evaluates the current board position and chooses the move most likely to lead to victory based on their knowledge - they don't need to see the entire future.
The performance measure is like exam marks - it objectively quantifies how well the agent achieved its goals, guiding all its decisions.
A learning agent is like a child who improves their behavior based on rewards and punishments from parents, getting better over time through experience.
